Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary:
The paper proposes Skip-Window or SkipW an abstraction encapsulating RNN cells to actively skip updates similar to some earlier works like Skip-RNN, Skim-RNN, and ThrRNN. The novelty of the method comes is in having control over the total updates to control the overall computational budget compared to previous methods which didn't provide deterministic upper bounds and varied depending on the inputs. The idea is very simple and straightforward and can be looked at as a logical extension to the Skip-RNN line of work combined with a windowed approach on time series as used in ShaRNN (Dennis et al., NeurIPS 2019). The entire time series is divided into Windows of length L (which is a tunable parameter) and each window has a precomputed (from the final hidden state of the previous window) per-time step (update inside the window) importance vector which can be used as an indicator to update or not to update following the binarization as done in previous methods. The strict sparsification of this per-window importance vector to have only K non-zeros per window helps reduce compute to an upper bound ratio of K/L. The method further uses another threshold term over the sparsified importance vector to control finer budget requirements if needed. The experimentation is done on 2 tasks HAR-2D-Pose (with 32 time steps) and Adding task with 50 timesteps. The evaluation shows that Skip-Window shows good performance./accuracy compared to previous flexible RNNs with a reduction in the total number of updates. Finally, the impressive part of the paper is the real-world evaluation on Jetson Nano with a more complex workflow involving pose estimation from images for HAR-2D-Pose. 

Pros:
1. Simple and elegant idea. SkipWindow solution is in theory generalizable to multiple RNNs without much hassle. The abstraction could be thought of as an independent outer layer over RNNs similar to most other works in the space. 
2. Related work section is very thorough and the claims are grounded.
3. The architecture is easy to understand along with the aspects of training
4. The experimentation on both datasets reveals interesting insights and showcase the advantage of SkipW over other methods in both accuracy and computational budget. 
5. I highly commend the deployment experiments and evaluating it with complex workflow and showing how skipping updates and inputs can help compute latency and resources.
6. Plots are very clean and appendix and ablation are good. 

Weaknesses:
1. While I feel the idea works well and is elegant. It still is a combination of a couple of known techniques (mentioned in summary) which limits the novelty somewhat. But that doesn't stop the method from being useful.
2. Figure 2 is not required or needs redesigning as the equations were much clearer than the figure and the gates just made it hard to understand. 
3. I understand while K/L upper bound is guaranteed, the authors want to use thr for finer control, I just feel, it might not be required for super long sequences. - Just a comment.
4. My major issues are with experiments. While I like the work on HAR-2D-Pose and agree it is a good choice of dataset. I also want to see the success of Skip W on long term dependency tasks. I wouldn't count adding tasks as it can be trivially solved using good initialization (Henaff et al., ICML 2016). While I appreciate the efforts, I would like to see results on at least one or two (at least two preferred but I understand the time limitations, so one good dataset also works for the discussion phase) more real-world datasets to check the generalization. It can be Keyword spotting, phoneme detection, or even noisy-CIFAR. Something around 100 timesteps or more would be great

I am asking the authors to add these things during rebuttal or give reasoning corresponding to it. This is a key question to be addressed during the discussion. Note that I do not need deployment for these datasets but would like to see these numbers in the paper for more datasets to make the paper stronger. The reasoning for the long-range is because the gains would be more profound there than in 32 time steps. 

5. While the K/L based on importance makes sense per window. Sometimes, RNNs tend to identify signatures randomly. I suggest the authors add a comparison with random K choice instead of top K choice per window and have multiple variants of it like periodic sampling or all inputs being from start or end (these are deterministic for prediction time). This experiment will help us determine if the importance vector actually contributes to the decision making on skipping updates. The rationale behind this comes from the action recognition literature where people have shown that random sub-sampling works decently well compared to intelligent sub-sampling. Another thing to add here is because the importance vector per window is chosen based on the previous window it might not be optimal and maybe random selection might work fine. If random sampling (even not so random like periodic etc.,) works well, then I am not sure about using importance vectors anymore.

Without these baselines, it is hard to argue otherwise. I strongly suggest authors pursue this to make the claims more solid. This is another thing that needs to be addressed during the discussion.

Decision:
Even though the novelty is slightly limited, I like the idea for its ease, decoupled natured and potential generalizability along with the control on computational budgets. I appreciate the authors for ablation and on-device experiments which are very thorough. The only issue I found with the paper is the experimentation and baselines. I want to see at least one long term dependency task (real-world) and baselines that evaluate if the importance vector is even needed with the simple sampling strategies among each window. 

I am very much willing to increase the score based on discussion and the improvements on the experimentation front.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Edit after rebuttal and discussion.

I thank the authors for extra experimentation to showcase the effectiveness of SkipW. While most reviewers here agree that the novelty is limited (that doesn't stop it from being useful), I strongly think the impact due to SkipW will be translated to the real-world. There has been some discussion on the datasets, which I agree are not extensive making the initial experimentation weak. However, the new experiments compensate to an extent and I would like to recommend a weak acceptance with a score of 6 (I am still between 6 and 7, waiting for other reviewers to pitch in).

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

**Summary:**
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), an innovative abstraction designed to encapsulate recurrent neural network (RNN) cells, enabling selective updates to actively skip computation. This approach draws inspiration from previous works such as Skip-RNN, Skim-RNN, and ThrRNN. The primary novelty of SkipW lies in its ability to enforce a deterministic upper bound on the total computational budget, a significant improvement over prior methods whose computational costs varied unpredictably based on input characteristics. The underlying concept is commendably simple and straightforward, effectively extending the Skip-RNN lineage by integrating a windowed approach, reminiscent of techniques used in ShaRNN (Dennis et al., NeurIPS 2019). The methodology involves segmenting the entire time series into discrete windows of a configurable length L. Within each window, a per-time step importance vector is precomputed, derived from the final hidden state of the preceding window. This vector acts as an indicator, guiding whether an update should occur, following a binarization process consistent with earlier literature. Crucially, the strict sparsification of this per-window importance vector, ensuring only K non-zero entries, guarantees a reduction in computation to an upper bound ratio of K/L. The method also incorporates an optional threshold parameter for finer control over budget requirements. Experimental validation was performed on two tasks: HAR-2D-Pose (with 32 time steps) and an Adding task (with 50 time steps). The evaluation demonstrates that Skip-Window achieves competitive performance and accuracy compared to other flexible RNN architectures, while simultaneously reducing the total number of updates. A particularly impressive aspect of the paper is the real-world deployment evaluation on a Jetson Nano, showcasing its effectiveness within a complex workflow involving pose estimation from images for the HAR-2D-Pose task.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Conceptual Simplicity and Elegance:** The core idea is both elegant and simple, rendering the SkipWindow solution broadly generalizable to various RNN architectures without substantial modifications. It can be conceptualized as an independent, external layer applied over existing RNNs, aligning with similar approaches in this domain.
2.  **Comprehensive Related Work:** The related work section is exceptionally thorough and effectively grounds the paper's claims within the existing body of literature.
3.  **Clarity of Architecture:** Both the proposed architecture and its training methodology are presented with remarkable clarity, making them easy to understand.
4.  **Insightful Experimental Findings:** The experiments conducted on both datasets provide valuable insights, clearly demonstrating SkipW's advantages in terms of both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency compared to competing methods.
5.  **Robust Deployment Evaluation:** The deployment experiments are highly commendable, particularly the evaluation within a complex real-world workflow (pose estimation) and on a constrained device like the Jetson Nano. This effectively illustrates how selective skipping of updates and inputs can significantly reduce computational latency and resource consumption.
6.  **High-Quality Presentation:** The plots are exceptionally clean, and the appendix, along with the ablation studies, are well-structured and informative.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Limited Intrinsic Novelty:** While the proposed idea is effective and well-articulated, it largely synthesizes a combination of existing techniques, which somewhat constrains its inherent novelty. Nevertheless, this does not diminish the method's practical utility.
2.  **Figure 2 Requires Revision:** Figure 2 appears either redundant or necessitates a significant redesign. The mathematical equations provide a much clearer explanation of the mechanism, whereas the visual representation, particularly the depiction of gates, introduces unnecessary complexity and hinders comprehension.
3.  **Ambiguity of Threshold Parameter:** While the K/L upper bound offers a guaranteed reduction, the necessity of the additional threshold (`thr`) for finer control might be questionable, especially when dealing with exceptionally long sequences. This is a minor comment rather than a critical flaw.
4.  **Insufficient Experimental Breadth:** My primary concerns lie with the scope of the experimental validation. While HAR-2D-Pose is a commendable choice, it is crucial to demonstrate SkipW's success on tasks characterized by *long-term dependencies*. The Adding task, unfortunately, is less compelling as it can be trivially addressed with effective initialization strategies (as noted by Henaff et al., ICML 2016). I strongly recommend including results from at least one, and ideally two, additional real-world datasets to strengthen the paper's generalization claims. Suitable examples could include Keyword Spotting, Phoneme Detection, or noisy-CIFAR, preferably involving sequences of 100 timesteps or more. The rationale is that the benefits of skipping updates would likely be more profound in such longer sequences. It is important to clarify that while these additional experiments are requested, on-device deployment for them is not necessary; simply presenting the performance numbers in the paper would suffice. This point is critical and should be thoroughly addressed during the rebuttal or discussion phase.
5.  **Absence of Baseline for Importance Vector Justification:** The current approach relies on an importance vector per window, derived from the previous window's hidden state. This may not always be optimal. To rigorously validate the contribution of this importance vector, I suggest comparing SkipW against baselines that employ simpler, deterministic sampling strategies for selecting K updates per window. This could involve, for example, random K selection, periodic sampling, or consistently selecting inputs from the beginning or end of each window. Insights from action recognition literature suggest that random sub-sampling can often perform surprisingly well compared to more "intelligent" sub-sampling schemes. Such an experiment would definitively ascertain whether the learned importance vector genuinely enhances the decision-making process for skipping updates. Without these comparative baselines, it becomes challenging to fully justify the use of the importance vector. This is another fundamental point that demands attention during the discussion.

**Overall Assessment and Initial Recommendation:**
Despite a minor limitation in its fundamental novelty, I find the proposed SkipW method highly appealing due to its inherent simplicity, its decoupled architecture, and its potential for broad generalizability, coupled with its effective control over computational budgets. The authors are particularly commended for their detailed ablation studies and the robust on-device experimental validation. My primary reservations, however, lie with the scope of the experimental evaluation and the absence of critical baseline comparisons. Specifically, the paper would be significantly enhanced by demonstrating SkipW's efficacy on at least one real-world task involving long-term dependencies and by including comparative analyses against simpler sampling strategies to ascertain the true value of the importance vector. I am genuinely open to raising my assessment score contingent upon a satisfactory discussion and tangible improvements in the experimental section.

---
**Edit after rebuttal and discussion:**

I extend my gratitude to the authors for conducting the additional experiments, which effectively showcase SkipW's capabilities. While a consensus among reviewers, including myself, acknowledges the method's somewhat limited novelty, we collectively agree on its significant practical utility. I firmly believe that the impact of SkipW will resonate strongly in real-world applications. Although the initial experimental setup was indeed somewhat constrained by the chosen datasets, the new results provided by the authors substantially mitigate these earlier weaknesses. Consequently, I am now inclined to recommend a weak acceptance. While I am still deliberating between a score of 6 and 7, I am currently leaning towards 6, awaiting further input from other reviewers.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

4. My major issues are with experiments. While I like the work on HAR-2D-Pose and agree it is a good choice of dataset. I also want to see the success of Skip W on long term dependency tasks. I wouldn't count adding tasks as it can be trivially solved using good initialization (Henaff et al., ICML 2016). While I appreciate the efforts, I would like to see results on at least one or two (at least two preferred but I understand the time limitations, so one good dataset also works for the discussion phase) more real-world datasets to check the generalization. It can be Keyword spotting, phoneme detection, or even noisy-CIFAR. Something around 100 timesteps or more would be great

I am asking the authors to add these things during rebuttal or give reasoning corresponding to it. This is a key question to be addressed during the discussion. Note that I do not need deployment for these datasets but would like to see these numbers in the paper for more datasets to make the paper stronger. The reasoning for the long-range is because the gains would be more profound there than in 32 time steps. 

5. While the K/L based on importance makes sense per window. Sometimes, RNNs tend to identify signatures randomly. I suggest the authors add a comparison with random K choice instead of top K choice per window and have multiple variants of it like periodic sampling or all inputs being from start or end (these are deterministic for prediction time). This experiment will help us determine if the importance vector actually contributes to the decision making on skipping updates. The rationale behind this comes from the action recognition literature where people have shown that random sub-sampling works decently well compared to intelligent sub-sampling. Another thing to add here is because the importance vector per window is chosen based on the previous window it might not be optimal and maybe random selection might work fine. If random sampling (even not so random like periodic etc.,) works well, then I am not sure about using importance vectors anymore.

Without these baselines, it is hard to argue otherwise. I strongly suggest authors pursue this to make the claims more solid. This is another thing that needs to be addressed during the discussion.

Decision:
Even though the novelty is slightly limited, I like the idea for its ease, decoupled natured and potential generalizability along with the control on computational budgets. I appreciate the authors for ablation and on-device experiments which are very thorough. The only issue I found with the paper is the experimentation and baselines. I want to see at least one long term dependency task (real-world) and baselines that evaluate if the importance vector is even needed with the simple sampling strategies among each window. 

I am very much willing to increase the score based on discussion and the improvements on the experimentation front.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Edit after rebuttal and discussion.

I thank the authors for extra experimentation to showcase the effectiveness of SkipW. While most reviewers here agree that the novelty is limited (that doesn't stop it from being useful), I strongly think the impact due to SkipW will be translated to the real-world. There has been some discussion on the datasets, which I agree are not extensive making the initial experimentation weak. However, the new experiments compensate to an extent and I would like to recommend a weak acceptance with a score of 6 (I am still between 6 and 7, waiting for other reviewers to pitch in).

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), an approach that allows RNNS to have improved computational efficiency at the cost of accuracy. SkipW adds a procedure to existing RNN cells that allows them to process fewer inputs while remaining in a strict computational budget. This work demonstrates the benefits of SkipW through experiments on multiple data sets.

This work proposes a structured procedure to process fewer inputs during inference while abiding by a computational budget. Each skip seems to be calculated over a window of inputs in a sequence, thereby minimizing inter and intra sequence variability in computation. This work does seem to have merit in a practical setting where the availability of computational resources can vary. The ability to manage dynamic budget, though not totally novel (ThrRNN also has the thr param), does provide an advantage with SkipW being perhaps more flexible with both K and thr usage.

This work empirically demonstrates the benefit of SkipW  over several baselines on two data sets. There are, however, some points that need to be addressed.





Figure 2 and equations 5-10 seem to be a bit unclear. Figure 1 and the first two paragraphs of section 3 seem to imply that  \hat{u}_{W} is calculated once as an L length vector at the beginning of an L sized window.   Here \hat{u}_{W}^{K}  are the selected K entries for the whole L sized window. Therefore what does \hat{u}_{W,t}^{K}  denote. 

In Figures 3 and 4 what is input processed % ? Is it K/L. For example, in Figure 3 and in the case of L=8, the setup K=1 implies K/L = 0.125 , but the K=1 point for SkipW is at less than 10% mark on the xaxis. Furthermore, how is it that K=[3-8] all have roughly the same input processed % ? Its the same case for K=[1-4] and K=[3-16]  


In Figure 4, the number of points for ThrRNN seem to be much more than SkipW. This seems to imply much more finer control over computational cost for ThrRNN as compared to SkipW (more coarser control). What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Figure 6 seems to imply that the inputs in p3 and p4 can be more easily dropped as compared to p1 and p2. What are the  authors' thoughts about the connection of this ease of dropping inputs to the various attention mechanisms that are available in literature. Furthermore, is it the case that later inputs will typically always have more tendency to be dropped in SkipW ?
 

Figure 7 seems to be a bit unclear. Is the accuracy being depicted the relative accuracy as compared to thr=0.4 and K=8? Furthermore, how is the accuracy being calculated as the thr,K are changing on the fly as time/sequences go on? This might imply that different thr,K values see different data? Is the same exact sample being used to calculate the accuracy? 


It seems that L-K denotes the minimum number of inputs that will be skipped (as there is a further 'binarize' that runs on the K inputs). Therefore, it seems that the system will under utilize the available computational capacity. This might not be desirable as any loss in accuracy might have been reduced if all available computational capacity was used. What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Perhaps a text processing task in NLP would have made the results stronger as in practical scenarios, this is one of the common modalities where RNNs are used.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), a novel approach designed to enhance the computational efficiency of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) by selectively processing a reduced number of inputs. This efficiency gain, however, comes with a trade-off in accuracy. SkipW integrates a specific procedure into existing RNN cells, allowing them to operate within a strict computational budget by intelligently skipping inputs. The authors empirically demonstrate the benefits of SkipW through experiments conducted on multiple datasets.

The work proposes a structured method for processing fewer inputs during the inference phase while adhering to a defined computational budget. A notable feature is that each skip operation is applied over a window of inputs within a sequence, which appears to effectively minimize computational variability both within and across sequences. This contribution holds practical merit, especially in scenarios where computational resources are dynamic. While the capability for dynamic budget management is not entirely novel (e.g., ThrRNN also features a 'thr' parameter), SkipW potentially offers greater flexibility through its combined usage of 'K' and 'thr' parameters.

Empirical evaluations showcase SkipW's advantages over several baseline methods across two distinct datasets. Nevertheless, several aspects require further clarification or discussion.

Firstly, the clarity of Figure 2 and equations 5-10 is somewhat lacking. Based on Figure 1 and the initial two paragraphs of Section 3, it seems that $\hat{u}_{W}$ is calculated once as an L-length vector at the beginning of an L-sized window, and $\hat{u}_{W}^{K}$ then represents the K selected entries for this entire window. Given this, the precise definition and role of $\hat{u}_{W,t}^{K}$ remain ambiguous and warrant further explanation.

Secondly, regarding Figures 3 and 4, the meaning of 'input processed %' needs to be clarified. Is this metric intended to represent K/L? For instance, in Figure 3, with L=8 and K=1, the expected K/L ratio is 0.125 (12.5%), yet the SkipW data point for K=1 is positioned below the 10% mark on the x-axis. Additionally, it is puzzling why ranges such as K=[3-8] (and similarly K=[1-4] and K=[3-16] in other contexts) consistently show approximately the same 'input processed %' values.

Thirdly, in Figure 4, the considerably higher density of data points for ThrRNN compared to SkipW suggests that ThrRNN might offer a more granular level of control over computational cost, while SkipW provides coarser adjustments. The authors' perspective on this difference in control granularity would be valuable.

Fourthly, Figure 6 suggests that inputs at positions p3 and p4 are more readily dropped than those at p1 and p2. It would be insightful to hear the authors' thoughts on how this differential ease of dropping inputs relates to existing attention mechanisms in the literature. Furthermore, is it a general characteristic of SkipW that later inputs in a sequence will consistently exhibit a higher propensity to be skipped?

Fifthly, Figure 7's presentation is ambiguous. Could the authors clarify if the displayed accuracy represents a relative measure, perhaps benchmarked against a specific configuration like thr=0.4 and K=8? Additionally, a detailed explanation of how accuracy is computed when both 'thr' and 'K' dynamically change over time or across sequences is needed. Does this dynamic adjustment imply that varying (thr, K) combinations are evaluated on different subsets of data? It is crucial to confirm whether the accuracy calculations consistently utilize the exact same sample set.

Sixthly, it appears that L-K represents the *minimum* number of inputs that will be skipped, given the subsequent 'binarize' operation applied to the K selected inputs. This suggests a potential underutilization of the available computational budget. Such underutilization might be suboptimal, as any observed accuracy degradation could potentially be mitigated if the full computational capacity were leveraged. The authors' perspective on this potential underutilization and its implications would be appreciated.

Finally, the inclusion of a text processing task, particularly from the Natural Language Processing (NLP) domain, could significantly enhance the robustness of the experimental results, given that RNNs are frequently deployed in such practical applications.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This submission presents an extension of SkipRNN, Skip-Window, that splits input sequences into windows of length L from which only K samples can be used. This guarantees that the computational budget is never exceeded. Skip-Window implemented this inductive bias by predicting L updating probabilities in parallel at the beginning of each window. L needs to be set prior to training, whereas K can be modified at test time. The model is evaluated in two tasks, namely a synthetic adding task and human activity recognition. Authors report latency and energy consumption in small platforms, showing the impact of this research direction in real applications.

My main concern regarding the proposed architecture is that it limits the types of skipping patterns that can be discovered -- whereas the original SkipRNN can in principle learn any such pattern. For instance, Skip-Window cannot produce the skipping patterns shown in Figure 6 in Campos et al. (2018) unless K=L or L=1 (in both cases, Skip-Window reduces to SkipRNN). This can even be seen in the results for the adding task, where Skip-Window is actually *not* solving the task for most values of K (c.f. Figure 4). Recall that the output distribution has a variance of 0.166, and Campos et al. define solving the task as achieving an MSE two orders of magnitude below such variance. The reason for this is that Skip-Window with 5<K<L will miss the second marker in some sequences, as it needs to guess its position -- which is random within the second half of the sequence. For K<5, there’s a chance that Skip-Window will miss the first marker as well. As a sanity check, I suggest that authors report the percentage of first and second markers that are missed in the adding task as a function of K. Plotting the MSE of an LSTM or GRU that skips inputs randomly, and varying the fraction of skipped inputs as in Campos et al., would also provide context for the presented error rates.

Despite the imposed constraints in terms of skipping patterns, Skip-Window can be useful in tasks where the input signal can be downsampled more uniformly. After all, the adding task is a challenging problem for RNNs that skip input samples as missing one of the markers will massively increase the error rate. This potential is shown in the human activity recognition (HAR) results. However, I believe that more experimental evaluation is needed before this paper can be published at ICLR. First, the input sequences for HAR are extremely short (32 timesteps), which makes it difficult to draw strong conclusions. Second, since the Skip-Window architecture limits the types of skipping patterns that the model can discover, it is difficult to claim that Skip-Window is a generic RNN architecture unless experiments on more domains are reported (e.g. sequential MNIST, NLP).

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This submission introduces Skip-Window, an innovative extension of the SkipRNN architecture designed to operate under strict computational constraints. The core idea involves segmenting input sequences into distinct windows of length L, from which only a predetermined number of K samples are ultimately processed. This methodology effectively guarantees that the computational resources remain within a defined budget. The model implements this structural constraint by concurrently predicting L update probabilities at the onset of each window. While the window length L must be established before training, the number of samples K can be dynamically adjusted during inference. The authors evaluate Skip-Window's performance across two distinct tasks: a synthetic adding task and human activity recognition (HAR). Furthermore, the paper provides valuable insights into the practical implications of this research by reporting latency and energy consumption figures on resource-constrained platforms.

My primary reservation concerning the proposed architecture stems from its inherent limitation on the diversity of skipping patterns it can discover. In contrast, the original SkipRNN framework is, in principle, capable of learning arbitrary skipping configurations. For instance, Skip-Window is unable to reproduce the complex skipping patterns illustrated in Figure 6 of Campos et al. (2018), unless K is set to equal L or L is set to 1, in which scenarios the architecture effectively degenerates into the original SkipRNN. This fundamental restriction is evident in the results for the adding task, where Skip-Window largely fails to solve the problem for most values of K (as depicted in Figure 4). To clarify, "solving the task" is defined by Campos et al. as achieving a mean squared error (MSE) that is two orders of magnitude below the output distribution's variance of 0.166. The underlying reason for this failure is that when 5 < K < L, Skip-Window frequently misses the second marker in certain sequences, as its position is randomly distributed within the latter half of the sequence. If K falls below 5, there is an increased likelihood of missing even the first marker. To further elucidate this behavior, I recommend that the authors include data on the percentage of first and second markers missed in the adding task, presented as a function of K. Additionally, providing context for the reported error rates would be beneficial by plotting the MSE of a standard LSTM or GRU that employs random input skipping, varying the fraction of skipped inputs in a manner consistent with Campos et al.

Despite these imposed limitations on discoverable skipping patterns, Skip-Window holds promise for applications where the input signal can be downsampled with greater uniformity. The adding task, after all, represents a particularly challenging scenario for RNNs that skip inputs, as omitting even a single marker can drastically inflate the error rate. This potential is indeed hinted at by the favorable results observed in the human activity recognition (HAR) task. However, I believe that a more comprehensive experimental evaluation is necessary before this paper can be considered for publication at ICLR. Firstly, the input sequences utilized for HAR are relatively brief (only 32 timesteps), which makes it difficult to draw robust, generalizable conclusions. Secondly, given that the Skip-Window architecture inherently constrains the types of skipping patterns that can be learned, it is challenging to assert its generality as an RNN architecture without presenting experimental results from a wider array of domains (e.g., sequential MNIST, natural language processing tasks).

title: Learning explanations that are hard to vary

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper investigates one possible pitfall of current gradient descent method that averaging gradients over different examples failed to capture the invariance between different examples, through it can learn quickly by memorizing data. The authors claim that this is one important reason that machine learning with GD can't generalize well to out of domain dataset. To solve this issue, this paper focus on developing a method to learning the invariant explanations. The authors firstly formalize the a notion of consistency of the loss surface and also propose a practical algorithm, AND-mask. The authors also carried experiments and analysis on synthetic dataset to validate the effective the method. Overall, the work is important and the paper is well presented. I vote for accepting. 


##########################################################################
Pros 
	1. The paper studied the important problem of model generalization. The paper is well motivated and the knowledge presented can benefit the community. 
	2. The paper defines the invariance as the consistency for minima of the loss surface. The author presented case study examples as well as math formulations. The presentation is clear and easy to understand.
	3. The authors validate the value of capturing invariance through the implementation of a practical AND-mask, and run experiment on a set of tasks. The baselines are reasonably chosen. The proposed method greatly outperforms other baselines. 
	4. The synthetic tasks unveil the difference between ILC and gradient averaging. It is very helpful for the readers. 

##########################################################################
Cons
	1. The work claim that they run experiments on a set of real-world tasks. The authors introduce artificial label noisiness in CIFAR-10 by randomly shuffling the labels. However, recently work (e.g https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.10528.pdf) found that noisiness exists in many popular datasets. It would be interesting to show that ILC can also handle such naturally introduced noises. 
2. What is the connection between invariance and model casual explanation?

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper addresses a fundamental challenge within current gradient descent methodologies: the potential inability of gradient averaging across diverse examples to effectively capture underlying invariances. The authors posit that this limitation contributes significantly to the poor generalization capabilities of machine learning models when faced with out-of-domain datasets. To tackle this, the work proposes a novel approach focused on learning invariant explanations. Key contributions include the formalization of a consistency notion for the loss surface and the introduction of a practical algorithm, termed AND-mask. The effectiveness of this method is subsequently demonstrated through experiments and analyses conducted on a synthetic dataset. Overall, the research presented is impactful, and the manuscript is well-structured and clearly articulated. I recommend acceptance.

##########################################################################
**Strengths:**

1.  The paper delves into the critical problem of model generalization, a highly relevant and impactful area within machine learning. The motivation for this work is clearly established, offering valuable insights that could significantly benefit the broader research community.
2.  The concept of invariance is rigorously defined as the consistency of minima on the loss surface. The authors adeptly combine illustrative case studies with precise mathematical formulations, resulting in a presentation that is both lucid and readily comprehensible.
3.  The proposed method's utility in identifying invariances is practically validated through the implementation of the AND-mask algorithm. Experiments conducted across various tasks demonstrate its efficacy, with a thoughtful selection of baselines against which the proposed approach consistently delivers superior performance.
4.  The synthetic tasks are particularly insightful, effectively highlighting the conceptual and practical distinctions between the Invariant Learning Concept (ILC) and conventional gradient averaging techniques, which greatly aids reader understanding.

##########################################################################
**Weaknesses:**

1.  While the paper claims to evaluate its method on "real-world tasks," the experiments on CIFAR-10 introduce artificial label noise via random shuffling. Recent literature (e.g., [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.10528.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.10528.pdf)) indicates that intrinsic noise is prevalent in many widely used datasets. It would substantially strengthen the empirical evidence to demonstrate how ILC performs when confronted with such naturally occurring noise.
2.  The paper could benefit from further elaboration on the conceptual link between the proposed notion of invariance and the broader field of causal explanations in machine learning models. A clearer discussion of this connection would enhance the theoretical context.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.rating: 9: Top 15% of accepted papers, strong accept
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors introduce and formalize the concept of Invariant Learning Consistency (ICL), which is motivated by the idea that "good explanations are hard to vary" in the context of deep learning. Instead of using the arithmetic mean to pool gradients (logical OR), the authors propose to use the element-wise geometric mean of gradients with a logical AND masking. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data sets are reported under the setup of supervised learning and reinforcement learning. 

This paper is well written and clearly presented. The exploration of using geometric mean with a logical AND masking to pool gradients in deep learning is very interesting and novel. The proposed method learns invariances in gradient-based optimizations and can help with the memorization issue. This work has made good efforts towards a better understanding of learning, memorization and generalization of OOD. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data sets have demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed AND-mask, comparing with commonly used regularizers and several baselines. 

As the authors point out, the AND-mask is one of multiple possible ways to improve consistency, and it is unlikely to be a practical algorithm for all applications. It would be great if the authors can provide further insights on what kind of data distributions (or applications) could potentially benefit from the proposed method versus using the arithmetic mean. In addition, it would be interesting to explore hybrid methods that combine the advantages of pooling gradients using arithmetic mean and geometric mean (i.e., AND-mask).

Rating:
9: Top 15% of accepted papers, strong accept
reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review:

Review:
This paper introduces and formalizes the concept of Invariant Learning Consistency (ICL), drawing inspiration from the principle that "good explanations are difficult to alter" within the domain of deep learning. The authors propose an innovative method for pooling gradients, substituting the conventional arithmetic mean (which implies a logical OR operation) with an element-wise geometric mean combined with a logical AND masking technique. The effectiveness of this approach is substantiated through experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets, across supervised learning and reinforcement learning paradigms.

The manuscript is exceptionally well-structured and lucidly presented. The exploration of utilizing the geometric mean alongside an AND masking mechanism for gradient aggregation in deep learning is both highly intriguing and fundamentally novel. This proposed method effectively fosters invariances during gradient-based optimization processes and offers a promising solution to mitigate the memorization issue commonly observed in deep networks. Furthermore, this research makes a significant contribution to a deeper understanding of learning dynamics, the challenges of memorization, and the crucial aspect of out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Empirical evidence from experiments conducted on diverse synthetic and real-world data strongly supports the efficacy of the proposed AND-mask, demonstrating its superior performance when compared against commonly used regularizers and various baseline methods.

As the authors themselves acknowledge, the AND-mask represents one specific strategy among several potential avenues for improving consistency, and its universal applicability across all applications is not anticipated. To further enrich the discussion, it would be beneficial if the authors could provide additional insights into the specific characteristics of data distributions or types of applications where their proposed method might offer distinct advantages over pooling gradients using the traditional arithmetic mean. Additionally, exploring hybrid methodologies that integrate the strengths of both arithmetic and geometric mean gradient pooling (i.e., the AND-mask) would present a fascinating and worthwhile direction for future investigation.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
this work posits that invariant mechanisms exist in a dataset. a machine learning algorithm that is trained using gradient descent usually averages gradients across examples. the thesis is that by averaging gradients, information is lost. the method posits that in a gradient descent algorithm, instead of an arithmetic average, a geometric (or karcher) mean can be used to preserve information about invariant mechanisms - while ignoring confounders. there are difficulties in a straightforward application of the geometric mean, so a simple heuristic algorithm is developed, involving masking gradients depending on whether the sign of the gradient agrees across a batch of examples (or, whether some agreement threshold is reached). this algorithm is tested on a synthetic dataset, a semi-synthetic task on CIFAR-10, and coinbase, an RL algorithm. 

recommend major revisions and baselines. it is interesting ideas and it would do the ideas justice to put a lot of effort into a rewrite so the ideas are properly understood by the ICLR community. clarity needs to be significantly improved in the introduction and conceptual framing (mainly in intro/methods sections)—the rest of the paper is largely well-written, the experiments are well-documented in the appendix, relationship to invariant risk minimization and causality is documented in appendix.

baselines: sum vs geometric mean is a well-known effect in products of experts vs sums of experts work. i think this should be the right baseline to compare to. train separate classifiers and use them as a product of experts, and you should get the same performance as in this paper. definitely need to discuss this related work and how learning consistency /geometric mean measures differ and warrant comparison.

the writing is more difficult to read than it ought to be. more work is needed to re-use existing concepts. for example, consistency is overloaded here (it might confuse some readers from a statistics background). the paper's title has the concepts of varying and explanations (variance and explanations do not appear in the method). small things like this abound. trying to find specific examples where i have a strong opinion to help:

* instead of consistency, it may be worth spending some time to think of another word that is less confusing. congruence? concordance? 'learning invariant mechanisms with geometric mean gradient descent' is an example of a title that would be easier to understand: (1) mechanisms relates to prior work such as the pearl work that is cited (indeed, in the appendix the authors state 'causality... is a key element informing our exposition'). a causal mechanism is a well-known concept to the ICLR community. (2) concordance is still overloaded (e.g. this is a major concept in biostatistics) but not to the degree that consistency is. similarly, if you choose congruence, this could have the nice geometric interpretation in the hessian example that is helpful for understanding the work in figure 3.

* the david deutsch quote distracts and confuses, remove it (i also know some researchers that might be immediately skeptical of a david deutsch citation in an ICLR paper). see above for ideas on title changes.

* the first example is helpful to understand the goals of the paper. however, figure 1 --  the symbol with the plus sign and arrow is confusing, all the axes tick labels need to be much larger

* the 'an example' chess example in the gray box on page 2 is unnecessary and distracting. instead, the discussion of the relationship to arjovsky et al could be inserted (with grass and cows), which is much more interesting and useful to a reader. 

* equation 1 engenders a double negative throughout the conceptual framing of the paper. i highly suggest considering renaming this to a 'consistency' score to reflect the name of the method; this will make it easier for readers to understand. 

* 'patchwork' may be another jargon word, try to find another one that is easier to understand. on page 3: 'low consistency of a classic patchwork solution' is loaded with jargon. first, a patchwork solution is not defined here or in previous work. second, a 'classic patchwork' solution is more confusing. removing adjectives such as 'classic' or even 'patchwork' throughout the paper may help readability and clarity. 

* i found the synthetic memorization dataset very difficult to understand. never start a sentence with math like p(y | x_{d_M}), it looked like it was connected to the previous with a \cdot. ideally write out the functional form of the mechanism. the axes in figure 5 are not labeled, so i have a hard time understanding what we are looking at: are the x and y axes d_S or d_M? how is environment A vs environment B defined? 

* re: wording -- ideally delete shortcuts. do not introduce more unnecessary lingo, because it leads to sentences like 'the shortcuts are not shared across environments, but provide a simple way to classify the data, even when pooling all the environments together'. this is asking a reader to do a lot of work: (1) what is pooling? this has a standard usage. (2) what are environments? this is used in RL (3) what are shortcuts, and how do they relate to environments? trying to rewrite this, i would want something with fewer novel concepts, that focuses on mechanisms that are invariant across datapoints or groups of datapoints (groups of data arising from causal graphs, e.g. in causal inference terminology).

* extremely minor, a few typos and inconsistent capitalization (section 3.1 is not capitalized, figure titles not capitalized, paragraph headings, figure legends not capitalized , good to pick one and stick to it)

Rating:
2: Strong rejection
reviews.text:
This work explores the concept of invariant mechanisms within datasets, proposing that traditional machine learning algorithms using arithmetic mean gradient descent inherently sacrifice valuable information. The authors suggest that employing a geometric (or Karcher) mean for gradients could better preserve information related to these invariant mechanisms while effectively mitigating the influence of confounders. Recognizing the practical difficulties of a direct geometric mean application, the paper introduces a heuristic algorithm that masks gradients based on whether their signs align across a batch of examples, or if a certain agreement threshold is met. This algorithm is evaluated on a synthetic dataset, a semi-synthetic task involving CIFAR-10, and within a Coinbase reinforcement learning environment.

**Recommendation:**
While the core ideas presented hold some intellectual appeal, the current manuscript requires such extensive revisions—particularly concerning its conceptual clarity, presentation, and the inclusion of fundamental experimental baselines—that it falls significantly short of publishable standards. Therefore, a strong rejection is warranted at this stage. To properly convey the interesting underlying concepts to the ICLR community, a substantial rewriting effort is indispensable.

**Major Points for Revision:**

1.  **Clarity and Conceptual Framing:** The paper's clarity, especially within the introduction and methodology sections, demands considerable improvement. The prose frequently complicates understanding, making the content unnecessarily difficult to comprehend.
    *   **Ambiguous Terminology:** The term "consistency" is used in a way that could easily confuse readers familiar with its specific meaning in statistics. It would be beneficial to explore alternative terminology, such as "concordance" or "congruence," which might reduce ambiguity. Adopting "congruence," for instance, could also provide a neat geometric interpretation that aligns with the Hessian example in Figure 3.
    *   **Misleading Title:** The current title, with phrases like "varying" and "explanations," does not accurately reflect the proposed method, as neither variance nor explicit explanations are central to the technique. A more descriptive title, such as "Learning Invariant Mechanisms with Geometric Mean Gradient Descent," would be more informative. The term "mechanisms" would also appropriately link to the cited works by Pearl, which the authors acknowledge in the appendix as a key influence on their discussion of causality.
    *   **Unnecessary Distractions:** The inclusion of the David Deutsch quote is distracting and potentially confusing. Its removal is strongly advised, especially given that some researchers might view such a citation with skepticism in an ICLR context.
    *   **Incomprehensible Figures and Examples:**
        *   While the initial illustrative example is helpful, Figure 1's symbol (a plus sign with an arrow) is unclear, and all axis tick labels need to be significantly enlarged for readability.
        *   The "an example" chess scenario in the gray box on page 2 is extraneous and detracts from the paper's focus. Replacing this with a discussion of the relationship to Arjovsky et al.'s work (e.g., the grass and cows example) would be far more relevant and insightful for the reader.
        *   Equation 1 contributes to a pervasive double negative in the paper's conceptual framing. Renaming this to a "consistency score" would greatly enhance reader understanding by aligning it with the method's proposed name.
        *   The term "patchwork" is introduced as undefined jargon, particularly in phrases like "low consistency of a classic patchwork solution" on page 3. The use of such loaded terms, especially with adjectives like "classic," creates unnecessary confusion. Defining new terms or opting for more universally understood language throughout the paper would significantly improve clarity.
        *   The synthetic memorization dataset (Figure 5) is exceptionally difficult to understand. Sentences should not commence with mathematical expressions like `p(y | x_{d_M})`, and the functional form of the mechanism should be explicitly detailed. Furthermore, Figure 5's axes are unlabeled, making it impossible to discern what `x` and `y` represent (e.g., `d_S` or `d_M`), or how "Environment A" versus "Environment B" is defined.
        *   The introduction of new, undefined terminology like "shortcuts" leads to convoluted sentences (e.g., "the shortcuts are not shared across environments, but provide a simple way to classify the data, even when pooling all the environments together"). This forces the reader to decipher multiple novel concepts simultaneously (pooling, environments, shortcuts, and their interrelations). The exposition should ideally focus on mechanisms invariant across data points or groups, drawing from established causal inference terminology if appropriate, rather than inventing new jargon.

2.  **Missing Baselines and Related Work:** A critical omission is the lack of comparison against relevant baselines. The distinction between sum and geometric mean is a well-established concept in the literature concerning "products of experts" versus "sums of experts." This body of work offers a direct and highly relevant comparison point that is currently absent.
    *   It is plausible that training separate classifiers and combining their outputs as a "product of experts" could yield performance comparable to the proposed method. This scenario necessitates explicit comparison.
    *   The paper must thoroughly discuss this related work and clearly articulate how its approach to learning consistency or employing geometric mean measures differentiates itself from and warrants comparison with these existing methods.

3.  **Minor Presentation Issues:**
    *   Several minor typos are present throughout the manuscript.
    *   Inconsistent capitalization is observed in section headings (e.g., "section 3.1" not capitalized), figure titles, paragraph headings, and figure legends. Adopting a consistent style for capitalization is recommended.

In summary, while the paper presents an intriguing hypothesis regarding invariant mechanisms and gradient aggregation, its current form suffers from fundamental issues in clarity, conceptual presentation, and experimental rigor, particularly concerning crucial baselines. The extensive revisions required to address these shortcomings necessitate a strong rejection.

**Rating:** 2: Strong rejection

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors attempt to recover invariant solutions, defined as those with a similar loss neighbourhood across environments. This concept is interesting, especially since it can be incorporated with a clever understanding of loss gradients and hessians which underlies the generality of the proposed approach. 

The presentation is puzzling to me. The objective for all intents and purposes is to recover the causal solution from data in multiple environments, but this is never explicitly mentioned even though causality is said to be a "key element" in the Appendix. In this area, also precise conditions are needed on data distributions from different environments which seem to play no role in this paper. A more thorough description of what can be expected from invariant solutions and when we are expected to consistently recover them is needed in my opinion. The introduction is therefore also vague, what do we mean by generalizing out-of-sample? Is the objective to ensure good performance with arbitrary shifts in distribution while maintaining a causal graph? Is there an assumption that the invariant mechanism is unique among available environments?

Regarding the ILC, hessians and gradients I do see they are related but it is not lear to me how changing hessians would lead to optimizing for the ILC. The ILC is defined with respect to all local minima, I am not sure how such an objective would be attainable. More details here would be helpful.

In general, I found all experiments highly constructed and nothing like what you'd expect to see in reality. If the objective is improved performance out-of-sample, why not evaluate the method on domain generalization tasks (office, caltech datasets, etc.)? Invariant solutions may also be defined as minimizing a worst-case optimization problem across environments, yet the robust optimization literature is not mentioned.

Minor comments:
- I may be misunderstanding, but as described in the Appendix, does the synthetic experiment have shortcuts for both labels sampled from the same distribution?
- With unobserved confounding, it is known that the causal solution does not have zero loss gradient (for example in a linear model, least squares returns biased parameter estimates), what can we expect in that case?


In summary, I do believe the proposed approach to be interesting but the context in which it is expected to work, as well as its relationships with causality, robust optimization and domain generalization are not well described; this prevents me from raising my score at this moment.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces an intriguing concept for identifying "invariant solutions," defined as models exhibiting a consistent loss neighborhood across various environments. This approach holds considerable promise, particularly due to its innovative integration of loss gradients and Hessians, which appears to form the basis for its potential generality.

However, the overall presentation of the work raises several points of confusion. While the implicit objective of the methodology seems to be the recovery of causal solutions from data collected in multiple environments, this aim is never explicitly articulated in the main text, despite causality being identified as a "key element" in the Appendix. Furthermore, the paper lacks a detailed discussion regarding the precise conditions required for data distributions across different environments, which seems to be a critical component for the proposed approach yet remains largely unexplored. A more thorough exposition of what precisely constitutes an invariant solution and under what circumstances its consistent recovery can be reliably expected would significantly enhance the clarity of the paper. Consequently, the introduction itself suffers from a degree of vagueness; for instance, what exactly is meant by "generalizing out-of-sample"? Does this imply ensuring robust performance under arbitrary distributional shifts while simultaneously preserving the underlying causal graph? Is there an underlying assumption that the invariant mechanism is unique across the available environments?

Regarding the Invariant Loss Curvature (ILC) concept, while the relationship between Hessians, gradients, and the ILC is conceptually understandable, the precise mechanism by which modifications to Hessians would lead to the optimization of the ILC remains unclear. Given that the ILC is defined with respect to *all* local minima, the practical attainability of such an optimization objective warrants further explanation. Additional details in this section would be highly beneficial.

From an empirical perspective, the experiments presented appear to be highly constructed and bear little resemblance to real-world scenarios. If the primary goal is to demonstrate enhanced out-of-sample performance, it would be far more convincing to evaluate the proposed method on established domain generalization benchmarks (e.g., Office, Caltech datasets). Moreover, the concept of invariant solutions often aligns with minimizing worst-case performance across environments, a principle extensively explored within the robust optimization literature, which is conspicuously absent from the paper's discussion.

Minor comments:
*   Could the authors clarify whether the synthetic experiment, as described in the Appendix, inadvertently introduces "shortcuts" where labels for both components are sampled from the same distribution? This could potentially influence the experimental outcomes.
*   In scenarios involving unobserved confounding, it is a known fact that the true causal solution may not necessarily correspond to a zero loss gradient (e.g., in linear models, least squares typically yields biased parameter estimates). What are the expected implications or behaviors of the proposed method under such conditions?

In summary, while the core idea presented in this work is undeniably interesting and possesses significant potential, its specific operational context and its intricate connections with established fields such as causality, robust optimization, and domain generalization are not adequately described. This lack of comprehensive contextualization currently prevents me from recommending a higher score.

**Rating:** 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Graph Edit Networks

reviews.confidence: 1: The reviewer's evaluation is an educated guess
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Graph Editing is a prominent research area which overlaps a variety of fields in computer science. As a typical example, a series of graphs - each obtained from its predecessor - can naturally represent the evolution of a system over time. From this viewpoint, it would be natural to obtain a means of predicting how such a series of graphs will evolve over time. The authors introduce a simple output layer (called "GEN") that can be used in graph neural networks to do precisely that.

On the theoretical side, the authors show that GENs have several useful properties, such as the ability to provide a constant-factor approximation of the Graph Edit Distance problem (assuming that a suitable teaching signal is provided). On the empirical side, the authors demonstrate the capabilities of GENs via several experimental evaluations, comparing the results to those of existing time series prediction approaches such as variational graph autoencoders. The experimental results are mostly encouraging: unlike existing systems, GENs can predict node insertions, and GENs also achieved higher accuracy for predicting edge operations.

The paper is well written. While the introduction and definitions are aimed mostly at researchers who have some knowledge on the topic (and hence I could not verify the details of the results), I believe that the paper would be of interest to the ICLR community. From what I have seen and understood, I think the contribution is also sufficient to warrant a presentation at the conference.

Questions:
-In Theorem 1, isn't it important to clarify (in the statement) that the translation can be carried out in polynomial time?
-Is there a specific reason for using the "Edit Cycles" and "Degree Rules" systems in the experiments? Both seem like rather arbitrary choices. Would it be possible to use GENs for systems where, e.g., we simply delete an edge incident to a highest-degree vertex in the graph? Or the highest-degree vertex?
-I'm curious whether GENs could be used to develop a neural network capable of learning to solve classical problems such as computing Treewidth... that problem can also be formulated as a graph editing task (via the elimination ordering characterization).

Minor remark:
Page 6: "GENs capability..." should maybe be "the capability of GENs..."; also, "Experiments are reported in three groups cover" should be "Experiments are reported in three groups and cover"

Post-Rebuttal Update:
I acknowledge having read the author's response and I also glanced over the new experiments.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
The field of graph editing holds significant importance across various domains within computer science. A compelling application involves modeling system evolution as a sequence of graphs, where each subsequent graph is a transformation of its predecessor. The ability to forecast how such graph sequences might progress is highly desirable. This paper addresses this need by proposing "GEN," a novel output layer designed to integrate with graph neural networks for predicting graph evolution.

From a theoretical perspective, the authors demonstrate that GENs possess several advantageous characteristics. Notably, they establish that GENs can achieve a constant-factor approximation for the Graph Edit Distance problem, provided an appropriate teaching signal is utilized. Empirically, the paper effectively showcases GENs' capabilities through a series of experimental evaluations. These experiments include comparisons against established time series prediction methods, like variational graph autoencoders. The findings are largely positive: GENs exhibit a unique ability to predict node insertions, a feature absent in current systems, and also demonstrate superior accuracy in forecasting edge operations.

The manuscript is clearly articulated. Although the introductory material and definitions appear to target an audience with existing familiarity in the subject matter (which prevented me from independently verifying all technical specifics), I am confident that this work will resonate with the ICLR community. Based on my understanding and review, the presented contribution is substantial enough to merit inclusion as a presentation at the conference.

**Points for Clarification and Discussion:**

*   For Theorem 1, it seems crucial to explicitly state within the theorem's formulation that the translation process can be executed in polynomial time.
*   Could the authors elaborate on the rationale behind selecting the "Edit Cycles" and "Degree Rules" systems for the experimental validation? These choices strike me as somewhat arbitrary. It would be insightful to know if GENs could be applied to simpler, perhaps more intuitive, evolutionary rules—for instance, scenarios where an edge connected to a highest-degree vertex is removed, or even the highest-degree vertex itself.
*   I am particularly curious about the potential for GENs to be leveraged in developing neural networks that could learn to solve classic graph problems, such as computing Treewidth. This problem, notably, can be framed as a graph editing task through its elimination ordering characterization.

**Minor Editorial Suggestion:**

*   I have a couple of minor stylistic suggestions for page 6: "GENs capability..." would likely read better as "the capability of GENs..."; furthermore, "Experiments are reported in three groups cover" should be corrected to "Experiments are reported in three groups and cover".

**Post-Rebuttal Update:**

Following the rebuttal period, I have reviewed the authors' response and briefly examined the supplementary experimental results.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
-------
This paper proposes a model that, given one graph, predicts a sequence of edits that transforms this graph to the next one in a sequence of evolving graphs. To this end, it proposes the graph edit network (GEN), which is a linear output layer that transforms node embeddings to a set of scores that are then used to deterministically select graph edits. The space of edits predictable by this model covers the full space of graph changes. The paper moreover shows that, given a graph matching and a pair of graphs, we can algorithmically find a near-optimal graph edit sequence for generating training data. Finally, the paper demonstrates the model's capabilities on a set of synthetic benchmarks.

Strong points
------------------

+ The paper is well written and properly embedded in the literature.
+ The approach of solving graph transformation by predicting a complete sequence of graph edits (i.e. including deletion) is novel and certainly very interesting.

Weak points
------------------

- I am not really convinced by the approach. There is no way for the proposed model to know if it is predicting step K+1 of the ongoing edit sequence or step 1 of a new sequence (put differently: the model has no memory/is stateless; unlike to e.g. existing recurrent graph generative models). There is also no signal telling the user that the edit sequence is finished. So it seems to me that neither the model nor the user can know which edit steps are actually predicted graphs and which are just intermediate states. 
- The experiments only use synthetic toy datasets and a single baseline each. There are no comparisons with recently published models using the common, but supposedly inferior method of generating the next graph from scratch.
- Theorem 1 does not "prove that graph edit networks can approximate the NP-hard graph edit distance" or show that "edits predicted by the GEN can be sharply bounded to be close to optimal", as (incorrectly) claimed in the introduction/abstract. The theorem merely shows that the space of possible edits is complete and that the edit sequence length of your training data can be bounded if you have an (NP-hard) optimal graph matching. There is no guarantee whatsoever on what the GEN might predict. Also, GEN doesn't use any mapping, so the statement "provided that an optimal mapping is used" is rather confusing.
- Theorem 1 merely transforms the graph edit problem into a graph matching problem. This is stated on page 5, but considering that graph matching is still NP-hard, the importance of Theorem 1 seems to be overstated throughout the rest of the paper. Especially since it is largely just a variant of Proposition 1 by Bougleux et al. 2017 (as cited).
- The hinge loss with margin is rather common in deep learning, see e.g. [1]. The novelty of the proposed loss function is thus significantly overstated. [Side note: Its description on page 6 also seems overly complicated. I would recommend that you simplify this.]
- The paper states that the model can achieve linear instead of quadratic runtime if the number of edge edits are limited. While this might be true, there is no measure on how such a constraint would affect model performance. The experiment described in the last paragraph of the experimental section only provides some data on scaling behavior, not on accuracy. Especially considering that this restriction limits the number of edits by the model and thus breaks its universality it can severely affect the outcome. [Side note: The number of non-negative edge filter scores actually does not need to be constant, it merely needs to scale with sqrt(N) instead of N. So you actually have more wiggle room than you allow yourself.]

Recommendation
--------------
I recommend rejection, mainly because of significantly lacking experiments and overstated theoretical results. The presented theoretical results are interesting but do not really give deep insights about proposed approach (specifically Theorem 1). The overall theoretical contribution is significantly smaller than the authors make them out to be.

Feedback for the authors
------------------------
I really appreciate the novel approach of predicting a complete instead of a limited edit sequence. I would recommend acceptance if you a) significantly extend the experiments to include real (ideally practically relevant) graph sequences and directly compare to multiple relevant methods, which you already mention in the related work, b) solve the stopping problem mentioned above (i.e. distinguishing between another and the first edit step), and c) make your statements more accurate and avoid overstating your results. Since especially points a) and b) are unlikely possible to be solved within 2 weeks I strongly encourage you to build on this work and submit a better version at the next conference.

[1] Junbo Zhao, Michael Mathieu, and Yann LeCun. Energy-based generative adversarial network. ICLR 2017
